data.title: Donald Trump Says No Money Will Flow To Iran Until His Demands Are Met — Then Leaves Situation Room Without Announcing A Decision On Proposed Deal
data.description:
On Friday, President Donald Trump left a White House Situation Room meeting without announcing whether he would approve a proposed agreement aimed at extending a fragile ceasefire with Iran.

Trump Sets Tough Conditions For Iran Deal

The development was reported by CNBC, citing an administration official. The White House did not immediately respond to Benzinga's request for comments.

Earlier Friday, Trump said on Truth Social that he would make his "final determination" after the meeting, while laying out a series of conditions Iran must meet before any agreement moves forward.

Those demands included preventing Iran from ever obtaining a nuclear weapon, keeping the Strait of Hormuz open to unrestricted shipping traffic and ensuring Iran's enriched nuclear materials are removed and destroyed under international oversight.

"No money will be exchanged, until further notice," Trump wrote, while adding that several lower-priority issues had already been resolved.

Despite the high-profile meeting, no final decision was announced afterward.

Iran Pushes Back On US Claims About Ceasefire Terms

Confusion surrounding the draft agreement deepened after Iranian state-linked media disputed several of Trump's assertions, the report noted.

Iranian sources also reportedly argued that releasing roughly $12 billion in frozen assets remains central to any agreement.

The temporary framework under discussion would reportedly extend the existing ceasefire, remove maritime threats in regional waters and create space for broader negotiations over nuclear activity, sanctions relief and frozen assets.

Oil prices fell 1.28% to $87.76 per barrel as of 6:16:54 p.m. EDT, while natural gas slipped 0.37% to $3.273 as of 6:17:09 p.m. EDT, with both commodities trading lower late Friday.

Treasury Says Economic Campaign Is Crippling Tehran

Meanwhile, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said Friday that Washington's pressure campaign has severely weakened Iran economically.

"We have seized about a billion dollars of their crypto," Bessent said during an interview with FOX Business. "Just outright grabbed the wallets."

Bessent described the broader strategy, dubbed "Operation Economic Fury," as pushing Tehran toward a financial crisis.

"They are at the end of their tether now financially," he said, citing inflation, funding shortages and mounting economic stress inside Iran.

Disclaimer: This content was partially produced with the help of AI tools and was reviewed and published by Benzinga editors.

data.title: Kevin O'Leary Says If You've Got $500K Saved, You Can Live Off the Interest and Do 'Nothing Else' To Make Money — 'It's All About Lifestyle'
data.description:
There is a certain fantasy that lives rent-free in almost every office cubicle in America. It usually starts around 2:17 p.m. on a Tuesday and sounds something like this: What if someone could just stop working forever?

According to "Shark Tank" investor Kevin O'Leary, that number might be lower than many people think.

"You can live off half a million bucks in the bank and do nothing else to make money," O'Leary said in a YouTube video in 2023. "It's all about lifestyle."

The longtime investor argued that someone with $500,000 invested conservatively could generate roughly 5% annually in fixed income with "very little risk." For investors willing to tolerate more market swings, he added that returns could climb closer to "eight and a half, nine percent" with some exposure to equities.

Don't Miss:

Then came the warning label, delivered in classic Mr. Wonderful fashion.

"Do not invest in your brother's restaurant or a bowling alley or a bar or all that other crap," O'Leary said. "You'll lose your money on that."

The $500K Retirement Fantasy Comes Down To One Word

Lifestyle.

That was the entire point of O'Leary's argument, and it is where the conversation gets more realistic.

That breaks down to roughly:

  • About $4,160 per month at a 5% return
  • About $5,620 monthly at an 8.5% return
  • Nearly $5,830 monthly at a 9% return

In lower-cost parts of the country, especially for retirees with a paid-off home, little debt and modest spending habits, that can absolutely support a comfortable lifestyle.

Trending: Make your first trade—or your next one—with commission-free investing and up to $1,000 in stock from SoFi.

But comfortable and luxurious are two very different zip codes.

Someone trying to maintain a Manhattan penthouse lifestyle on $50,000 a year may need to keep that LinkedIn profile updated indefinitely.

Paid-Off Homes Change The Math Fast

One major reason retirement numbers vary so wildly is housing.

A retiree with no mortgage, low property taxes and minimal debt can stretch income dramatically further than someone still carrying a large monthly housing payment.

Healthcare also matters.

That is one reason many financial planners still lean toward the more conservative 4% withdrawal framework instead of assuming consistent 8% to 9% annual returns forever.

Using that traditional guideline, a $500,000 portfolio would generate closer to $20,000 annually before Social Security.

The Real Risk Is Usually Not The Stock Market

Ironically, O'Leary's biggest warning had nothing to do with stocks.

It was about emotional investing.

Many retirees and near-retirees get tempted by risky "sure thing" opportunities pitched by relatives, friends or business partners. Restaurants, bars and small businesses may sound exciting, but they also carry extremely high failure rates.

O'Leary's broader message was simple: protecting wealth often matters more than chasing flashy returns.

For retirees trying to figure out whether $500,000 is enough, consulting a financial advisor can help run realistic projections based on taxes, housing costs, healthcare, inflation and lifestyle goals. For some households, an annuity or conservative bond strategy may make sense. Others may need more equity exposure or a delayed retirement timeline.

Because the real answer is not whether $500,000 is "enough."

It is whether someone's lifestyle expectations match the math.

data.title: 'Is Bankruptcy Really That Bad?' — Woman Took Out $90K HELOC to Pay Off Some Credit Cards but Still Owes $45K on Others
data.description:
A family bringing in a combined $151,000 annually is facing the bleak reality of being completely broke. 

In a post on Reddit's r/Debt community, a mother of four broke down her household debt, illustrating how even upper-middle-class incomes are failing to keep pace with the costs of housing, credit cards and childcare. 

"I am not making enough to service let alone tackle debt," she wrote. "Is bankruptcy really that bad?"

The couple's monthly obligations paint a picture of a household stretched past its limit: 

  • Mortgage: $300,000 balance ($2,750 per month)
  • Home equity line of credit, or HELOC: $90,000 balance ($690 per month)
  • Credit card 1: $10,000 ($400 per month)
  • Credit card 2: $20,000 ($600 per month)
  • Credit card 3: $15,000 ($465 per month)
  • Auto loan: $25,000 balance ($490 per month)
  • Unspecified liability: "Thousands" in unpaid medical bills

Between the mortgage, HELOC, three credit cards and a car payment, the family is shelling out $5,395 every month just to meet minimum requirements. 

Don't Miss:

On paper, the household income appears healthy, with the wife earning $66,000 per year and her husband bringing in $85,000. But after standard federal, state and payroll tax deductions, their take-home pay is far less because of taxes and health insurance deducted from their checks.

Let's say the amount they take home monthly is $9,520. After paying their mandatory debt service of $5,395 they have $4,125 left.

But feeding a family of six, paying utility bills, putting gas in the car and paying for childcare for four kids, means they are likely running a deficit each month. 

The average U.S. family spends about 20% or more of their annual income on childcare, with 20% of families spending upwards of $30,000 annually, according to Care.com's 2026 Cost of Care Report.

Desperate to get ahead of the problem the family tapped into a $90,000 HELOC to pay off other credit cards, according to the woman posting on Reddit.

Trending: Arrived Home's Private Credit Fund’s has historically paid an annualized dividend yield of 8.1%*, which provides access to a pool of short-term loans backed by residential real estate with just a $100 minimum.

Using a HELOC is often viewed as a debt consolidation move, according to financial move. But in this family's case, it shifted unsecured credit card debt into a secured loan tied directly to their primary residence, while a fresh $45,000 in credit card debt accumulated elsewhere, according to the post. 

With no remaining liquidity, the woman asked the community, "Is bankruptcy my best option at this point? How bad is it really? Would I keep my house and car? Would the HELOC be forgiven or just the cards?"

Many in the community suggested she contact a bankruptcy attorney to counsel her because every situation is unique. Although bankruptcy may be a viable path to relief, it's not likely to wipe the slate clean. 

What Are the Family's Options?

Because their $151,000 combined income is likely above the median income even for a household of six, they would probably fail the "means test" to file for Chapter 7 liquidation bankruptcy. 

Instead, they would be steered toward Chapter 13 bankruptcy, often referred to as a wage earner's plan or reorganization. 

According to Chapter 13 guidelines outlined by the Federal Courts of the United States, the process would impact their assets and debts in the following ways: 

The Takeaway

Using home equity to put a Band-Aid on a systemic cash-flow problem often transforms manageable unsecured risk into a direct threat to a family's housing security. While a Chapter 13 filing could eliminate their $45,000 credit card debt and wipe out their medical bills, it will require a court-mandated, tight budget for the next five years just to keep a roof over their heads. 

With credit card balances and minimum payments continuing to rise, some borrowers are exploring debt relief options before turning to bankruptcy. Accredited Debt Relief helps qualified consumers evaluate potential solutions for unsecured debt, including credit cards and medical bills.

data.title: Teen, 18, Paying Rent To Parents Gets His Room Taken By Relatives So He Moves Out Without Notice — Now They Say He's 'Overdramatic' And 'Childish'
data.description:
There is a special kind of family drama that begins the moment an 18-year-old starts paying rent. Suddenly, the phrase "my house, my rules" collides headfirst with "then why am I paying for the room?"

That was the situation one teenager described in a post on Reddit after moving out of his parents' home without warning when they once again told him to give up his bedroom for visiting relatives.

The 18-year-old wrote that he had spent years being the designated family sacrifice whenever guests from his father's side came to stay. While his three younger brothers kept their rooms untouched, he said he was repeatedly pushed onto the couch so relatives could sleep in his space.

"People from dad's family stayed over a lot too," he wrote. "So there were a lot of times I had to sleep on the couch for them."

Don't Miss:

He said the arrangement became even more frustrating because his brothers were never asked to do the same. Sometimes, he claimed, extra mattresses and blow-up beds would even be added to his room while everyone else's space stayed protected.

The teenager said things appeared to improve after multiple conversations with his parents. Then came a major change: he turned 18, started working full time and began paying rent.

According to the post, the family had an understanding that if he was now paying for the room, he would no longer be expected to hand it over whenever relatives visited.

That agreement lasted about as long as leftover holiday pie.

"A bunch of family visited a month ago and my parents told me to give up my room for them to have space," he wrote.

When he pushed back, the teenager said his parents reminded him it was still "their house."

So he made a decision that stunned them.

Trending: Everyone's watching EV stocks — fewer are paying attention to the lithium powering them

The Exit That Changed Everything

Instead of arguing further, the teenager called his maternal grandparents and asked whether he could move in with them.

"They said yes immediately," he wrote.

According to the post, his parents initially assumed he was bluffing until they watched his belongings being carried out to his grandparents' car.

"My parents told me I couldn't just leave and I was paying rent so I needed to give them real notice," he wrote. "I told them if I was kicked out of the space I was renting then I was just going to leave."

Since moving out, he said his parents have accused him of being "overdramatic" and "childish." They also told him the sudden departure made visiting relatives feel awkward and unwelcome.

Reddit commenters, however, largely sided with the teenager.

"They can't have it both ways," one commenter wrote. "Either it's their house and their decision, in which case you're free to decide you don't want to live there anymore, or you're paying rent for that room and they respect that space."

Another commenter praised the way he handled the situation.

"You didn't yell, you didn't cause a scene, you just found other arrangements and left," the person wrote. "Very adult if you ask me."

When Rent Changes The Parent-Child Dynamic

The story connected with many readers because the situation felt painfully familiar. Plenty of families struggle once adult children begin contributing financially at home and the line between "family rules" and "tenant expectations" starts getting blurry.

Charging rent can help teach responsibility and prepare children for independent living. But expectations often shift once money enters the equation. What once felt like a family favor can suddenly start feeling more like a landlord-tenant dispute with extra emotional baggage attached.

The teenager himself seemed aware of that distinction.

"I saw no reason to make a bigger deal out of it," he wrote in a follow-up comment. "Only now I have the ability to do something about the unfairness."

The fight was never really about a bedroom. It was about boundaries, respect and what happens when someone paying for a space realizes they no longer have to stay where they feel unwanted.

For parents balancing household finances, college costs, rising living expenses and the realities of adult children staying home longer, consulting a financial advisor can help families create clearer plans around rent, savings expectations and long-term housing goals before money tensions quietly turn into relationship problems.

data.title: If You Invested $100 In Ross Stores Stock 10 Years Ago, You Would Have This Much Today
data.description:
Ross Stores (NASDAQ:ROST) has outperformed the market over the past 10 years by 2.12% on an annualized basis producing an average annual return of 15.75%. Currently, Ross Stores has a market capitalization of $76.29 billion.

Buying $100 In ROST: If an investor had bought $100 of ROST stock 10 years ago, it would be worth $434.77 today based on a price of $232.01 for ROST at the time of writing.

Ross Stores's Performance Over Last 10 Years

Finally -- what's the point of all this? The key insight to take from this article is to note how much of a difference compounded returns can make in your cash growth over a period of time.
